question: How does gin route requests through its middleware chain?

shellCommands: "C:\\Windows\\System32\\WindowsPowerShell\\v1.0\\powershell.exe" -Command "rg -n \"func \\(c \\*Context\\) Next\\(|func \\(c \\*Context\\) Abort\\(|abortIndex|type HandlersChain|func \\(engine \\*Engine\\) ServeHTTP|func \\(engine \\*Engine\\) handleHTTPRequest|func \\(engine \\*Engine\\) Use\\(\" gin.go context.go routergroup.go"
graph.answer:
Gin turns middleware into one flat `HandlersChain` per route, then executes that chain with `Context.Next()`.

- Middleware registration is additive.
  - `Engine.Use()` forwards to the root `RouterGroup` and also rebuilds the special 404/405 chains. See [`gin.go` line 340](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\gin.go#L340), [`gin.go` line 357](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\gin.go#L357), [`gin.go` line 361](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\gin.go#L361).
  - `RouterGroup.Use()` appends middleware to that group’s `Handlers` slice. See [`routergroup.go` line 65](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\routergroup.go#L65).
  - When a route is registered, Gin concatenates parent group middleware + route handlers into one slice via `combineHandlers()`. See [`routergroup.go` line 86](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\routergroup.go#L86), [`routergroup.go` line 241](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\routergroup.go#L241).

- Request handling is just “match route, assign chain, run chain”.
  - `ServeHTTP()` allocates/reset a `Context` and calls `handleHTTPRequest()`. See [`gin.go` line 662](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\gin.go#L662).
  - `handleHTTPRequest()` finds the route, sets `c.handlers` to the matched chain, then calls `c.Next()`. See [`gin.go` line 690](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\gin.go#L690), [`gin.go` line 722](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\gin.go#L722).

- Execution order is controlled by `Context.Next()`.
  - `Next()` increments the current index and runs each remaining handler in order. See [`context.go` line 198](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\context.go#L198).
  - A middleware calls `c.Next()` to yield to the rest of the chain, then resumes afterward.
  - `Abort()` stops the remaining handlers by jumping the index to `abortIndex`. See [`context.go` line 57](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\context.go#L57), [`context.go` line 217](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\context.go#L217).

- 404/405 handlers use the same mechanism.
  - Gin assigns `allNoRoute` or `allNoMethod` and calls `serveError()`, which itself calls `c.Next()`. See [`gin.go` line 751](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\gin.go#L751), [`gin.go` line 758](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\gin.go#L758), [`gin.go` line 764](C:\Users\samch\AppData\Local\Temp\samchon-graph-corpus\gin\gin.go#L764).

In practice, the chain is ordered like this:

`engine middleware -> group middleware -> route handler`

And once a handler calls `Abort()`, the rest of the chain is skipped.

If you want, I can also walk through a concrete example with nested groups and show the exact handler order Gin builds.
